era_context: The late 9th century continued to be marked by political fragmentation in Western Europe, the ongoing Huang Chao Rebellion in China, and the consolidation of Byzantine power under the Macedonian Renaissance. The Islamic Abbasid Caliphate was fragmenting into regional dynasties. This was a period of significant military and political instability across Eurasia, with long-term consequences for the political map of Europe and Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited sources)

events.title: Capitulary of Quierzy: Frankish Succession Law
events.description: King Charles the Bald of the West Frankish Kingdom issued the Capitulary of Quierzy (also called the Edict of Quierzy) in 877, which attempted to regulate succession and inheritance of fiefs. This legal document was significant because it formalized the hereditary nature of fiefs and attempted to stabilize the succession process in the fragmenting Frankish Empire. However, the capitulary was largely ineffective in preventing further fragmentation and civil conflict. It is considered an important step in the development of feudalism, as it recognized the hereditary claims of nobles to their lands.
key_figures: Charles the Bald (West Frankish King, r. 843–877)
events.certainty_note: The Capitulary of Quierzy in 877 is a confirmed historical event, with the text surviving in multiple manuscript copies.
cross_references: Part of broader development of feudalism in Western Europe

events.title: Death of Charles the Bald
events.description: Charles the Bald, King of the West Frankish Kingdom and Holy Roman Emperor, died on October 6, 877, at Aachen. His death marked the end of an era in Frankish politics and led to a succession crisis. Charles had attempted to consolidate power and stabilize the Frankish Empire, but his death precipitated further fragmentation. He was succeeded by his son Louis the Stammerer, whose weak rule and early death (879) would lead to further instability and the eventual emergence of the Capetian dynasty.
key_figures: Charles the Bald (West Frankish King, r. 843–877)

sources.name: Regino of Prüm, Chronicon, compiled early 10th century

events.certainty_note: The death of Charles the Bald on October 6, 877, is well-documented in contemporary Frankish annals.
cross_references: Led to 879-001 (death of Louis the Stammerer and succession crisis)

events.title: Huang Chao Rebellion: Continued Expansion
events.description: The Huang Chao Rebellion continued to expand its territorial control across northern and central China in 877. The rebel forces had established a quasi-state structure and were controlling significant portions of the Tang Empire's territory. The rebellion was causing widespread famine, displacement, and economic collapse. Contemporary sources report that the rebellion had attracted hundreds of thousands of followers, including peasants, bandits, and disaffected soldiers. The Tang Dynasty's ability to suppress the rebellion was severely compromised by the loss of tax revenue and military resources.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian by Sima Guang, compiled 11th century

events.certainty_note: The continuation and expansion of the Huang Chao Rebellion in 877 is well-attested in Chinese historical sources.

events.title: Byzantine Recapture of Bari
events.description: Byzantine forces, as part of the Macedonian Renaissance military expansion, conducted operations to recapture territories in southern Italy that had been lost to Arab and Lombard control. The recapture of Bari (in Apulia) was a significant achievement in the Byzantine effort to reassert control over the Mediterranean and southern Italy. This campaign was part of a broader Byzantine strategy to reclaim lost territories and suppress piracy and Arab raids that had flourished during earlier periods of Byzantine weakness.

sources.name: Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos, De Administrando Imperio, compiled 10th century

events.certainty_note: Byzantine military operations in southern Italy during the 870s–880s are documented, but the specific date of the recapture of Bari is uncertain. Some sources place it in 876, others in 877 or 880. The assignment to 877 is conventional.
cross_references: Related to 879-003 (Byzantine naval operations in the Aegean)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Capitulary of Quierzy has been debated by modern scholars; some sources place it in 876 or 878 rather than 877. The effectiveness and historical significance of the capitulary have been questioned by modern historians, who argue that it had limited practical impact on the fragmentation of the Frankish Empire. The date of the Byzantine recapture of Bari is disputed; some sources place it in 876, others in 880 or later. The scale of the Huang Chao Rebellion's expansion is difficult to verify from contemporary sources, and later chronicles may have exaggerated the numbers.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 877 is again heavily weighted toward East Asia (China) and Western Europe (Frankish Empire). The sources are primarily monastic chronicles and later compilations. The Byzantine Empire is documented through later compilations that are sometimes contradictory. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Islamic world are poorly documented for this specific year. Bias toward court-sponsored narratives and the perspectives of the elite means that the experiences of common people, merchants, and non-elite groups are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The Capitulary of Quierzy was issued by Charles the Bald in the year of his death
The death of Charles the Bald in 877 led to a succession crisis that was resolved with the death of Louis the Stammerer in 879
The continuation of the Huang Chao Rebellion in 877 is part of the broader rebellion that lasted from 875–884
Byzantine military operations in southern Italy and the Aegean were occurring simultaneously
